title: Berry pavlova
subtitle: A crisp meringue shell with a soft marshmallow centre, topped with cream and summer berries
excerpt: Pavlova is a large meringue baked slowly until the outside is crisp and hollow-sounding but the centre stays soft and chewy. Piled with whipped cream and fresh summer berries, it is a British summer centrepiece. The crust cracks when you cut it, which is correct.
sourceNotes: Pavlova was created in Australia or New Zealand in honour of the Russian ballerina Anna Pavlova, who toured both countries in the 1920s. The original recipe has been adapted extensively in British baking, where it became a standard summer party dessert. The British version typically uses cream of tartar to stabilise the whites and vinegar to create the marshmallow centre. This recipe follows the established British formula.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The meringue shell can be made a day ahead and left in the switched-off oven overnight. Fill with cream and berries no more than 30 minutes before serving.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven only, not fan; fan dries the meringue too fast and can crack the shell

glossaryTerms.definition: Adding a teaspoon of white wine vinegar and a teaspoon of cornflour to the meringue mixture is specific to pavlova. The vinegar strengthens the protein bonds in the egg white foam and the cornflour holds moisture in the centre during the long, slow bake. Together they produce the pavlova's characteristic marshmallow interior. Plain meringue with neither additive will bake through to a dry, crisp texture throughout.

Any trace of fat, including egg yolk or residue from previous use, will prevent egg whites from whipping to their full volume. Wipe the bowl and beaters with a clean cloth dipped in a little white wine vinegar before use. Use a metal or glass bowl rather than plastic, which can harbour invisible fat residues.

content.text: Pavlova lives or dies on the meringue shell. The outside should be crisp and white; the inside should be soft and slightly sticky. The two additions that set pavlova apart from an ordinary meringue are a teaspoon of white wine vinegar and a teaspoon of cornflour. Ensure
before you begin; even a drop of yolk or water will prevent the whites from whipping properly.
are added at the end of whipping. Without them the centre will bake to a dry, brittle texture rather than the soft, chewy marshmallow that defines a pavlova.

Heat the oven to 120 degrees C (conventional, not fan). Draw a 22 cm circle on a sheet of baking paper and flip it ink-side down onto a large baking tray. In a scrupulously clean bowl, whisk the 4 egg whites and quarter teaspoon cream of tartar on medium speed until the mixture forms soft peaks. Add the 220 g caster sugar a tablespoon at a time, whisking constantly on high speed. The meringue should be very thick and glossy after all the sugar is in. Sprinkle over the 1 tsp cornflour, 1 tsp vinegar and half a teaspoon vanilla and fold in briefly.

Pile the meringue onto the circle and spread to fill it, creating a slight hollow in the centre with raised edges. Bake for 1 hour 15 minutes until the outside is dry and set and the shell sounds hollow when tapped on the base. Turn off the oven and leave the meringue inside to cool completely with the door ajar, at least 2 hours or overnight. Do not open the oven during baking.

Transfer the cooled meringue shell to a serving plate. Whip the 300 ml double cream to soft peaks and spoon it into the hollow. Pile the berries over the cream and serve within 30 minutes, before the cream and berries start to soften the meringue.

items.problem: Meringue weeps (beads of sugar syrup on the surface)
items.solution: The sugar was added too quickly, leaving some undissolved. Rub a small amount of the meringue between your fingers; it should feel smooth, not gritty. Add the sugar one tablespoon at a time over at least 5 minutes of whipping.
The oven temperature was too high, or the fan was on. Pavlova needs slow, gentle heat to dry without cracking. Reduce the temperature to 110 degrees C on the next bake and use conventional heat only.

items.problem: Centre is liquid or completely unset
items.solution: The meringue needed more time in the oven. If the outside is set but the centre is still wet, give it another 15 minutes. Leaving it in the switched-off oven overnight is the most reliable way to get a fully set centre.
